questions.explanation: The EVM (Ethereum Virtual Machine) is the runtime environment that executes smart contracts on Ethereum.

questions.body: Which consensus mechanism does Ethereum currently use?
questions.explanation: Ethereum switched from Proof of Work to Proof of Stake with The Merge in September 2022.

questions.body: What is the maximum supply of Bitcoin?
questions.explanation: Bitcoin has a hard cap of 21 million coins, enforced by its protocol.

questions.body: What is a gas fee in Ethereum?
options: A fee paid to miners/validators for processing transactions
A fee charged by crypto exchanges
A penalty for failed transactions only
A fee for storing data on IPFS
questions.explanation: Gas fees compensate validators for the computational work required to process and include transactions in a block.

questions.body: Ethereum was co-founded by Vitalik Buterin.
questions.explanation: True — Vitalik Buterin co-founded Ethereum and published the whitepaper in 2013.
False — Bitcoin is pseudonymous. All transactions are publicly visible on the blockchain and can be traced.

questions.body: Smart contracts can be modified after deployment.
questions.explanation: False — Smart contracts are immutable once deployed. Upgradeability requires proxy patterns as a workaround.

questions.body: A wallet's private key can be derived from its public key.
questions.explanation: False — Public keys are derived from private keys via one-way elliptic curve cryptography, not the reverse.

questions.body: Which token standard is used for NFTs on Ethereum?
questions.explanation: ERC-721 is the standard for non-fungible tokens. ERC-20 is for fungible tokens, ERC-1155 supports both.
DeFi — Decentralised Finance — refers to financial services built on public blockchains without intermediaries.

options: When a single entity controls more than half of a network's mining/staking power
When 51% of token holders vote to fork a chain
A vulnerability in ERC-20 token contracts
When a smart contract drains 51% of a liquidity pool
questions.explanation: A 51% attack occurs when a single actor gains majority control of consensus power, allowing them to rewrite recent transaction history.
